speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: One, two, three, four, five, six, seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Good. Maybe you-- can you count from one to ten in Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, 10, better.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is August 24, 1995. Survivor is Robert Ness. Last name at birth was Nussbaum. Interviewer is Elliot Lefkovitz. It's in the city, state, Skokie, Illinois, United States. And the language of the interview is English. The date is August the 24th, 1995. My name is Elliot Lefkovitz. I am interviewing Robert Ness in Skokie, Illinois, United States. Let's begin with your name, your current name, and its spelling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Robert David Ness. N-E-S-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth and its spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name at birth was Berl-- B-E-R-L-- Nussbaum, N-U-S-S-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Present age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Present age.

speaker: interviewee
text: Present age is 65. That is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the--

speaker: interviewee
text: 66, I'll be-- that's right. 66 I'll be in October. 65.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birthplace, the city or town where you were born, and its spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Small town in Poland, Raczki-- R-A-C-Z-K-I. At that time, it was county Augustow, A-U-G-U-S-T-O-W. And the voivodeship was Bialystok, B-I-A-L-Y-S-T-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And voivodeship was the province?

speaker: interviewee
text: The province, correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: The province of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was 16 provinces before the war. Bialystok was one of the-- one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames or other names at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Berushek. That's how they used to call me. B-E-R-U-S-H-E-K. Or Bushek, B-U-S-H-E-K. That's when I was a child. That's how they used to call me. But they used to call me Berushek all the way through until we are separated from-- from there in 1940s. Oh, they-- Berushek was the way it-- people knew, and my friend knew, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a sort of a diminutive of Berl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diminutive, yes, of Berl. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's start out a little bit with you-- your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- how many were in your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The immediate family were five plus my aunt that was living with us. She was not married. So it's really six. My father, mother, eldest sister, Pola, that was killed on June 29, 1942. My sister, Liza-- she lives here in Northbrook-- and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and Sarah was my father's sister. She was single. And she was staying with us, living with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's name was Hirsh-- H-I-R-S-H-- Nussbaum. And my mother's name was Gita, G-I-T-A. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you, then, were the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest. It's correct. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as a-- a more-- as an extended family, was there an extended family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was. A grandmother from-- from my father's side, Chana. But she died-- I was a child, about five, six. So she must have died around '35. I know it was past the marketplace. That we used to have market days, I think, on Mondays and Thursdays. So she must have died on a Wednesday. I remember that there was a funeral, and I went too. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then there was cousins and aunts in different places, different cities. There was a cousin and aunt in Suwalki.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Suwalki was not that far.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also a county seat. But not our county. We belonged to Augustow. Suwalki was about 15 kilometers from our little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any family get-togethers for holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure, sure. Of course. I-- I remember, for example, Passover always was a very happy holiday. And we used to sing the songs. And my sisters were kind of going ahead of me in the [HEBREW] with the different melodies. And they used to go fast. And before you know it,  it used to be kind of a little struggle. I remember once hitting-- took a cork from a bottle, threw it, broken glass. Because they were running so fast, I couldn't held up with them. But yes, it was a Jewish family. It was a warm Jewish family. Going to shul on-- on Saturdays, every Saturday. And on holidays, of course. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything special about the shul, or about the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember my father-- you know, there they used to kind of sell their [HEBREW] for holidays. I remember Father always used to have Shishi-- which is six-- just before the Maftir, before the reading of the Haftorah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he used to be called for an [HEBREW] on the holidays, and the high holidays too. I remember that. He used to be Shishi-- meaning six-- for the reading. And I remember the school. There was like what you would call a Hebrew day school. And that's where you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I went. But it lasted only for a few years. We used to have the subjects. I think the Hebrew was in the morning, if I'm not mistaken, and the Polish and general subject were in the afternoon, or the reverse. But I do remember that we had both. But it lasted only for a few years, and then I was taken out of school because they closed-- the school was closed. And I went to the general public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The language in your home was Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. Yiddish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also spoke Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Russian at all, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt taught us some songs in Russian, nursery songs like [RUSSIAN]. I taught it to my daughter, as a matter of fact. It-- it's about that-- that hunter that--

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Peter and the Wolf?'

speaker: interviewee
text: Something along those line, about a hunter. It-- it goes-- [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] The hunter-- the hare went for a walk. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] And suddenly, a hunter runs out. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] And he shoots straight into the hare. [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] My hare is dying. So that was the only Russian that I knew before the war. That was the only one. I didn't know any of the other part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any Simchat Torah celebrations in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sure, used to come in a flag in school, with an apple over-- over the flag, and being carried on the shoulders of either Father or somebody else. Yes, oh absolutely, the hakafot was a whole big production.

speaker: interviewer
text: These processions around the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Processions, yes, sure. The reading of the Torah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of business was your father in?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was exporting geese to Germany for Christmas, around Christmas times. And he also used to export timber to Germany. But that lasted only until Hitler came to power. I think he might have gone once-- not even once, perhaps. But he, around '34, '35, I think, that-- he stopped it with exports to Germany. Keep in mind, we were only six kilometers from the border, from the east Prussian border. And he used to export, also, I think, turkeys to England. But these were not live ones. But basically, the livelihood was a textile store that we had. Now, you have to understand that textiles there, it wasn't finished clothing and so on. Each one used to come and buy so many meters for a suit or for a dress or whatever it is. And then that particular person went to his own tailor, and he had it done, tailored for him to it. It was only in the bigger cities that you saw garments, finished ones, and so forth. Otherwise, it was all tailored by local tailors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was involved with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was my mother's business. And she was involved in it. But Father was involved too. I remember when geese-- the farmers used to bring the geese, and Father used to buy geese. So he used to give them a little kind of receipt, saying-- and he used to send to Mother, to go ahead and be paid. And then they used to come to the store. So sometimes they used to spend the money, or they didn't spend the money. My mother used to give them the money. But that's how it worked. It-- Father didn't carry too much money in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of activities outside of school were you involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- it's-- I was involved, I know, I went twice in summer camps. And that was a Betar organization. We had a bookkeeper who used to come, an accountant.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a-- a right-wing Zionist organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Well, in Poland, it wasn't-- yes, it was from the right of center, that-- insofar as Poland was concerned. It-- well, you would call it right because it was Betinski was a leader of that. And this particular accountant, he suggested for a camp, a summer camp, to be sent to around Seinai, which was closer to the Lithuanian border. Suwalki, Seinai, next to the Lithuanian border. And I spend there the summer of '37 and '38, where I was, in the camp. I was the youngest one, of course, as usual. And-- so that was the organization. Keep in mind, I was only seven, eight years old. So that was about the extent of that one. That-- but the youth group is-- was Betar I was in. There wasn't any meetings or anything of the kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particular hobbies or interests at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a-- kind of an-- pretty much a stamp collector at the time. I used to collect stamps. My mother used to write letters on behalf of people whose children were in the United States or in Uruguay or Argentina or-- So they used to get letters from their family. And so I used to wind up getting the stamps. It kind of got me an interest in geography, history. And so that was some of the things. But then I used to also, when the-- the day school was closed, the Jewish day school-- or Hebrew day school, whichever you want to call it-- I used to get private lessons. The teacher used to come. And it used to be daily. I used to have two hours of Hebrew, literature too, already started with Bialik and some other writers. And the homage. And on Friday, of course, I had to sing the whole of-- the whole Maftir. The whole of Torah, the whole prayers to the-- what you would use it here only for bar mitzvahs or bat mitzvahs, I used to do it every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the regular Haftorah chanter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, absolutely. It was just nothing to it. I mean, it wasn't that you were studied for years and years for your Haftorah. You just did it every week. And when you came to call, you were Maftir. Nobody had to teach you nothing. You knew it already because you were doing it every week, 52-- 52 weeks a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the relations like between the-- the Jews and the non-Jews in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally never felt any kind of animosity or any sense of anti-Semitism or so on. I wasn't affected by it as much. I remember in the '30s, at one time already, somebody came to school to pick me up because there was some trouble where there were some rabble-rousers or somebody there, that's about-- there was some big sign across the street, 'Don't buy from Jews.' But that was already in '30-- '36, '37. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: After-- after Pilsudski's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Pilsudski's death, right. Right. Pilsudski was pretty decent and very nice to the Jews. But that was already the influence with Hitler, too. You know? There was-- keep in mind, we were a small Jewish community, about 50 families or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had your family lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, must have been a long, long time because my father was born there. My mother was born there. Now you're talking 19-- end of 19th century. So they were around there for quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there ever any talk or thought given to immigration?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, Father used to say, 'My America is here.' I think after talking to my sister, she kind of indicated to me that Daddy was worried about that he will be like a deaf and mute person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'll-- Yes. Because he-- the language, he was afraid and worried about the language. I'm surprised at him. But he knew-- he knew German. He knew Polish. But be that as it may, supposedly that was one of the reasons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And maybe my sister, the eldest, would have come, perhaps, to this-- to the World Exhibit in 1939 in New York. But somehow or other, her cousin, he was religious and the military. So he went in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was the one who went, which was a cousin of ours from-- he's-- he served later on in the United States Army. He was wounded in France in the landings. And he lives now in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe later on, and there wouldn't have been a war, most probably my sisters maybe would have immigrated. I don't know. The fact is, in Polish, they have a saying-- [SPEAKING POLISH] Whether cat ate it or mouse ate it, the cheese is gone. You know, whichever the-- whatever the reason, the fact is we remained there, and then the war was upon us.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the 1930s, do you remember any talk at all at home about Hitler and the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. I was obviously either too young or they didn't discuss it with me. I know that the parents used to get the 'Der Moment,' which was a middle-center paper. Used to come from Warsaw, 'Der Moment'--

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish newspaper, a daily one. And they used to get it. I remember that. I remember there was not 'The Haynt.' 'The Haynt' was another-- 'The Haynt' means 'today.' 'The Haynt' was a little bit to the left. So I presume they were kind of in the middle. And the 'Der Moment,' used to get it. I know we saw a map of the colonies at the time around Tel Aviv. I remember [HEBREW] on a map, right next over the table there. So I had an ideas. It-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a Zionist interest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. Definitely, Mother. And I remember we had, in the living room-- ah, that's-- that's another story in itself. We had a copy, a reproduction of Ruben's [HEBREW]. That's-- it's a binding of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Isaac?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac. And it-- it-- it was really something. And one day, after the war already, I went to the [HEBREW]. And suddenly, in the slides, they showed-- oh, it was so-- I got to see it. I got to see it. So they went to the director. And believe it or not, let me in on the slide show, showed me. But it wasn't quite the one. It was from his students. The real true one was in the Hermitage. And when the Hermitage exhibit came here to Chicago, by gosh, I saw the original in the Chicago museum. It was really quite-- I was sitting and sitting, and crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Before we go on to the war itself, are there any-- in terms of a childhood memory, anything that you recall that-- that we haven't talked about? Either with the family or with other children in the town, or in school, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that after we went to the public school, no more the day school, I remember sometimes Polish boys ganging up on a Jewish boy who was kind of my age. And I kind of stepped in. I had a good relationship with the Polish boy. I didn't have any problems, really. None.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I kind of stepped away. I didn't have any problems myself in that respect. So that was part. I didn't hear any name callings, not to me. Maybe because of the fact because my mother was well known.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was known as a very generous person, a righteous person. You know, farmers used to come there, discuss with her families, what to do with daughters, with sons. So she was well known. I remember one. That was a good one. Oh, that's a beauty. As little boys, we used to run in wintertime. The farmers used to come in their sleighs. So here-- so we used to hang on in the back, kind of hiding, and take the ride on the sleigh, we're going it. And I remember once, and the-- when the farmers used to catch that somebody's behind, they used to take with their whip that they used for the horse, and hit you so you would get off the-- the-- the sleigh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember we run off. And then he asked, [YIDDISH], whose are you? I said, Gitki. Gitki, meaning my mother. He says, no, Gitki, [YIDDISH]. If it's Gitki, sit down. Then come in. So I never forgot that. Now, that is a good one, so a good question. So you see, that shows you the love and affection that Mother had. And I remember, too, Mother was very ill at one time. And a mass was said in the Catholic church on her behalf, for her to-- It was almost unheard of. I never heard of that anyplace on the subject. But she had a mass said for her, for her health. So she was quite generous and known in the whole place. And-- but this one with that farmer, [YIDDISH]. Whose are you? Gitki? Sit down!

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was arrive home with a big show. Those others were off, running off. And here, I got a ride in that sleigh like a prince.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember ever having-- you-- you said you were only a few kilometers from the border. Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: Six kilometers. I went to the border once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- were you ever in East Prussia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever visit? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Just up to the border, the Poland border was in a village called Lipowska-- L-I-P-O-W-S-K-A, Lipowska. It was six kilometers from our little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the late '30s, as the war clouds gathered, do you remember any tension at all, any build up of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember in '38, they were talking already, something about [INAUDIBLE] or something was-- it's-- it's-- it's the trains they used to collect money for defense. And I don't know what the-- I know that was in the-- the Jewish community was contributing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people used to come with the Rabbi. And oh, hey, how about some money for the-- for the defense forces of Poland? I remember that. And Father, of course, contributed too. So that was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember my elder sister, Pola, the one that was killed, she was going to a Polish gymnasium-- gymnasium, which was-- no-- Jewish girls weren't accepted so easily. There were maybe four or five all, but she was an excellent student, at  top.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Raczki?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- not in Raczki. She was going in Suwalki. There was no gymnasium in-- in Raczki. Raczki just had the grade-- grammar school. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was-- she used to come with friends. I remember once her school had an outing and they came, stopped at our house. And Mother was feeding them, and there was all those young girls-- food. It was all Polish girls. And they were just maybe three, four Jewish girls, and my sister among them. They were on an outing to the border in the woods, or something like that. So that was an example of Jews and Poles--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were-- did seem to get along.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know many-- I-- I see-- you know, I hear it many time, anti-Semitism and other stuff. I-- in Raczki, I did not feel it on my bones. Never did, in Raczki. I remember that they used to come pick me up from school, as I told you about it. But that was because supposedly trouble, and there was in-- some agitation about not to buy a Jew's. Now it's already coming, '37, '38, the influence of Hitler in Germany is most probably must have been-- already came in. And it's a small town, you know, where everybody knew one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: So maybe it was kind of-- you would have to bring almost in strangers or-- from another city or town. I'm looking for a towel. Here I had it, and I don't know where I put it. Oh! That's OK. Oh, never mind. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then, as the-- the-- the war came closer, and the summer of '39 and so on, do you remember anything about the Russian-German pact, the Molotov-Ribbentrop pact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's already when the war started. I didn't know about that, you know? I learned it later on in history. But at that time, I did not know about the Ribbentrop-Molotov pact of August 23, I think it was, 1939, or 22. And no, I knew nothing about it. And then suddenly, the war was started, and I was in Suwalki. Our-- our relatives, I was sick with the mumps or measles or something, a child disease. And-- and I remember Suwalki was bombed. Suwalki used to have those military barracks. It was not too far from the border, that under the Russians, it was already a military out-- it also had a big military post. Because it was also on the border with Germany. 1914, I'm talking about. And the same place remained on the border between Poland and Germany after the First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were--you were sent to Suwalki because--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was sent there away from the border. I suppose. Big deal, it was only 10 kilometers, 15 kilometers from it, you know? And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with which-- with which relatives were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with Brenner, B-R-E-N-N-E-R. In Poland, we spelled it, I think, with one N. But anyhow, that's [HEBREW]-- my mother's sister, she was married to Alter Brenner. And they were in Suwalki.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there. And then somebody came and said, the Germans were-- the police, or the Germans came into Raczki. But they were there just for three hours and moved out. Well. So then around September 6, I went back to Raczki. And I was already interested to see the Germans. Let's see how they look. What-- what is it all about? And then they came in, and the first they came wasn't even military. It was the gendarmes with the brown [INAUDIBLE] and the white ones. And they came. And the Jewish boys were running there to see, tried to talk to them. You know, everybody thinking, after all, he knows Yiddish. So he knows German, you know? So-- and me among them, of course. Me among them. So we-- but-- and then the Germans-- The Germans had been there in World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Of course, that's right. The people that knew the Germans-- that-- was nice, and gentlemen. You-- as a matter of fact, one of the great honors of-- of Raczki, what they had, was a picture of-- just a second-- either Hindenburg or Ludendorff-- I think it's Hindenburg, taking a piss right in the square of the town next to the car that they were chauffeuring him. So that was a big honor that the city got from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Urinating right in the center of the-- of the town. He was passing it. Passed it. You better keep in mind, in 1915, you know, there was this big push of the Russian into Eastern Prussia. And then they were move-- pushed out. And so he must have been there. Anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was really nothing, no-- as far as the expectations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, people that remembered the Germans from the First World War, people that dealt with Germany before Hitler, it was-- was such a nice country, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the--

speaker: interviewee
text: But my parents must have known about it because they knew about the refugees that were dumped on Poland on the borders in 1938. And that they dumped prior citizen or something of-- of Poland, who didn't have German citizenship, and let them sit there. And finally-- so that was a question of raising money. But I didn't know about it. I just learned it later, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or during the war from stories. At that time, I didn't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, at that time, were a child of 10 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Nine. Not even 10. The war started, I was nine. I was just about 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.
